creator: Anonymous
description: 1981.08.01 MTV First Four Hours Remastered from 12am Saturday August 1st, 1981.  This is a DVD containing remastered Video and Audio of the first 4 hours of MTV. You will find the full DVD which includes menus, chapters, and 2 audio options, his remastered audio and the original VHS. Also included are the 3 video streams extracted from the DVD all of which contain the two audio streams. Be sure to use a video player than can select different audio tracks! Original author is only known as Anonymous, 20120619.  About the Video The first 48 minutes and 17 seconds were pulled from a poor VHS bootleg, but the rest of the footage came from a copy made from the original umatic cassettes. Video is interlaced 30i. There is even a video smoothness test on the DVD! About the Audio More than 90% of the music videos are in full quality stereo sound. Live concert footage, or alternate takes not present on studio releases are in their VHS audio. See below for some excerpts or view the Notes and Trivia.txt file in this collection. The Files in this Collection VIDEO_TS.zip - The DVD. Contains all the original menus, chapters to each video, and some extras Notes and Trivia.txt Extracted video files 1981.08.01 - MTV First Four Hours Remastered - 01 - Original Broadcast - 12am Saturday August 1st, 1981.mpg 1981.08.01 - MTV First Four Hours Remastered - 02 - Videos Only - 12am Saturday August 1st, 1981.mpg 1981.08.01 - MTV First Four Hours Remastered - 03 - MTV Footage Only - 12am Saturday August 1st, 1981.mpg Below is an excerpt from included text file which details some information about this Remastered collection. Here are some interesting notes and trivia about the inaugral MTV broadcast, as well as this remastered version. === Blatant screw-ups during the broadcast: (00;30;25;28) About 15 seconds of "Take it on the Run" play and then the video goes blank and there is a tone.  Engineers quickly started playing a prerecorded bumper to compensate. (01;36;49;28) Fades to Robert Palmer's "Looking for clues", then fades back to the VJ by mistake, then fades back to the video. (01;47;41;03) Rod Stewart can be heard, but the video is completely blank. Engineers stop the video after 10 seconds and go to the next one. (02;01;37;01) Pat Benatar's "I'm gonna follow you" starts nearly 15 seconds into the video. === Blatant screw-ups in this reconstruction: At 02;51;50;15, the tape I had access to was damaged and there is a dropout and picture corruption that lasts about five seconds. The right audio track on the tape I had access to was either damaged in storage or improperly recorded.  As a result, the left audio track constitutes the majority audio presence.  This is not normally a problem as all of the studio and commercial audio was mono in nature, and roughly half of all music videos on that broadcast day were mono as well.  The only time this is a real problem is during some of the MTV reports, where you can't make out what someone is saying because the majority of their audio was in the right channel.  To try to compensate for this, I've included an additional audio track where the music for the videos came from stereo sources, perfectly synced to the video. The only footage I did not have access to is a complete version of Rupert Hine's "Surface Tension" video.  What I have is there, and then for the missing piece I had to use a VH1 Classics rebroadcast that someone had uploaded to the web.  This section is clearly introduced as not original footage, and there is a VH1C logo and timecode overlaid on it, so there is no mistaking it as original. The first 48 minutes of footage is from a 3rd-generation copy; after that it is all 2nd generation taken from a umatic casette.  This is why the video quality is sub-optimal for the first 48 minutes, and then gets better (in the middle of the Split Enz video, right where we leave the two "thugs"). The enhanced stereo track was the result of a reasonable effort -- if stereo sources were easily obtainable, they went on the track.  If they were very difficult and/or costly to obtain (such as ordering a $40 rare promo LP from another country and then trying to find someone local with a record player), I did not go through the effort.  As a result, the following videos do not have enhanced stereo audio: - PH.D. "Little Susie's On The Up" - The Cars "Double Life" (the performance in the video is unique to the video   and does not match their album session) - Blotto "I Wanna Be A Lifeguard" - The Pretenders "Talk of the Town" (The performance in the video does not   match any of their full-length albums, most discernable in Chrissy's last   "you've changed" lyric at the end of the song.  It is very likely that the   only performance that exactly matches the video is from their Extended Play   EP, which I do not own and could not easily find.) - All videos that were taken from live concert footage === Video changes compared to their album sessions: Both The Who videos have audio that is roughly 4% slower than the master studio session.  This is because they were edited to "25fps film" PAL video standards, and the conversion to NTSC was done by simply slowing down the film to 24fps. "You Better You Bet" was also trimmed by 40% to get it closer to a 3 minute running time (the original song is 5+ minutes). The Styx "Rockin' The Paradise" video actually spans the first two tracks of their album Paradise Theater. Phil Collins' "In The Air Tonight" has some stanzas chopped out to make a shorter video. Gerry Rafferty's "Baker Street" was chopped up all over the place.  I like this song, so I chopped up the studio session to match it (even the really obvious cut at 01;57;57;23 present in the original video). Robert Palmer's "Looking for Clues" has a nearly minute-long chunk taken out of the middle of the song to get the video length down to a reasonable running time. The Silencer's "Remote Control" and "Illegal" were edited down *significantly* in order to cram two different songs into a single video.  They also mixed in crowd noise over the edited studio session track.  The enhanced stereo audio track does not contain this fake crowd noise.  At one point, the edit turns a 4-beat measure into a 3-beat measure.  I was not happy editing the stereo track to this thing, but I did it because I came across the original fairly easily, and also because I find The Silencers an interesting footnote in New Wave history. Lee Ritenour's "Is It You" has a few stanzas cut out to trim it down by about 30 seconds, plus another 30 seconds off of the end. Blondie's "Rapture" has two chunks taken out of the studio session in order for the video to be under 5 minutes in length. Rainbow's "Can't Happen Here" has a stanza taken out of it.  Also, the last two minutes of the song aren't played, opting for a fade out instead. There is a Wikipedia page that lists all of the videos for the entire first day, going well beyond what this footage provides: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_music_videos_aired_on_MTV The umatic cassettes used to gather this footage were statically stored for so long that some print-through occured.  If you listen very carefully in quiet VJ sections, you can hear the audio start, faintly, before the video portion starts, then everything starts for real.  For more on why this happens, visit http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Print-through Despite the live crowd footage at the beginning, middle, and end of Styx's "Best of Times" video, the actual soundtrack of the video is NOT from a live concert but is matched to the studio session.  Special thanks to the now defunct blog http://digivinyltal.blogspot.com/ for The Silencers samples.  I don't understand why people close down blogs; it doesn't cost them anything to keep them running... The very first shot of "Stop Dragging My Heart Around" is full 30i video, then the rest of the video is 24p film.  Go figure. Looking for the original VHS recording found on the internet? Here it is: https://archive.org/details/1981.08.01_MTV_First_Four_Hours_12am_Saturday_August_1st_1981
